elements.sent-2411-001: School of Computing and Data Science The University of Hong Kong.
elements.sent-2411-002: As object detection becomes integral to many safety-critical applications, understanding its vulnerabilities is essential.
elements.sent-2411-003: Backdoor attacks, in particular, pose a significant threat by implanting hidden backdoor in a victim model, which adversaries can later exploit to trigger malicious behaviors during inference.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, current backdoor techniques are limited to static scenarios where attackers must define a malicious objective before training, locking the attack into a predetermined action without inference-time adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-005: Given the expressive output space in object detection—including object existence detection, bounding box estimation, and object classification—the feasibility of implanting a backdoor that provides inference-time control with a high degree of freedom remains unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-006: This paper introduces AnywhereDoor, a flexible backdoor attack tailored for object detection.
elements.sent-2411-007: Once implanted, AnywhereDoor enables adversaries to specify different attack types (object vanishing, fabrication, or misclassification) and configurations (untargeted or targeted with specific classes) to dynamically control detection behavior.
elements.sent-2411-008: This flexibility is achieved through three key innovations: (i) objective disentanglement to support a broader range of attack combinations well beyond what existing methods allow; (ii) trigger mosaicking to ensure backdoor activations are robust, even against those object detectors that extract localized regions from the input image for recognition; and (iii) strategic batching to address object-level data imbalances that otherwise hinders a balanced manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-009: Extensive experiments demonstrate that AnywhereDoor provides attackers with a high degree of control, achieving an attack success rate improvement of nearly $80\%$ compared to adaptations of existing methods for such flexible control.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{Fig/headline.pdf} \caption{AnywhereDoor provides attackers with flexible control over object detectors. Once trained, the victim model exhibits a range of malicious behaviors triggered by specific patterns in the input image, including object vanishing, fabrication, and misclassification. } \label{fig:headline}
elements.sent-2411-010: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have revolutionized object detection~\cite{Girshick__Rich, Ren_2017_Faster, Redmon_2016_You, Redmon_2018_YOLOv3, Carion_2020_End-End , powering applications across autonomous vehicles~\cite{Feng_2021_Deep, Choi_2019_Gaussian, Ucar_2017_Object , surveillance systems~\cite{Huang_2008_real-time, Mishra_2016_Study, Joshi_2012_Survey, Jha_2021_Real , medical imaging~\cite{Kaur_2022_Survey, Li_2019_CLU-CNNs, Yang_2021_Artificial , and beyond~\cite{Cheng_2016_survey, Lee_2017_Real-Time, Roy_2023_WilDect-YOLO, Usamentiaga__Automated .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: As these applications are often safety-critical, recent research efforts have shifted from solely improving detection accuracy to addressing security vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-012: Among the various threats to DNNs, backdoor attacks are considered one of the serious threats in the industry~\cite{Li_2022_Backdoor, Kumar_2021_Adversarial .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In such attacks, a victim model appears to function normally until a secret pattern, such as a small white patch, is presented, which then causes the model to misbehave intentionally.
elements.sent-2411-014: Most backdoor research has centered on image classifiers, leaving two gaps in understanding object detection's vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-015: First, existing studies generally assume a static, highly restrictive attack scenario where the attacker predefines a single malicious behavior and implants a corresponding trigger~\cite{Li_2022_Backdoor (\eg, using a small white patch as the trigger to make any nearby object disappear).}
elements.sent-2411-016: It remains unknown whether it is possible to design a backdoor that allows attackers to adapt their intended misbehavior dynamically based on context on the fly.
elements.sent-2411-017: Second, an intuitive approach to achieve dynamic behavior (i.e., implanting multiple triggers, one for each possible malicious behavior~\cite{Chan_2023_BadDet, Luo_2023_Untargeted, Chow_2024_Imperio, Doan__Marksman ) is impractical for object detection.}
elements.sent-2411-018: This is due to the model's large output space, which involves detecting the existence of a variable number of objects, estimating their bounding boxes, and classifying their semantics.
elements.sent-2411-019: These raise an intriguing question: do backdoor attacks on object detection always have to be constrained to a small number of predefined objectives? In this paper, we answer the above question by tackling these gaps and introducing AnywhereDoor, a backdoor attack that adapts to the attacker’s intent in real-time, allowing dynamic control over the victim model’s behavior.
elements.sent-2411-020: As illustrated in~\cref{fig:headline , once implanted, AnywhereDoor enables an attacker to selectively make objects disappear, fabricate them, or mislabel them, whether across any (untargeted) or specific (targeted) classes.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The same trigger can be applied to any inputs (\eg, real-time video streams) to achieve a consistent attack.
elements.sent-2411-023: This flexibility is achieved through three key modules within AnywhereDoor, each addressing a unique challenge in backdooring object detection.
elements.sent-2411-024: First, object detectors are multi-task learners, creating an exponential increase in possible attack combinations.
elements.sent-2411-025: We introduce an objective disentanglement method that reduces this complexity.
elements.sent-2411-026: Second, object detection relies on localized regions, so we propose a trigger mosaicking technique to ensure the trigger remains effective even if only a sub-region is processed.
elements.sent-2411-027: Third, object detection datasets often exhibit object-level imbalance, which can lead to biased manipulability across classes.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our strategic batching technique redistributes learning opportunities, enhancing attack efficacy across all classes.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our contributions are threefold: (1) We present AnywhereDoor, the first backdoor attack enabling flexible control over object detection models; (2) We introduce trigger mosaicking and strategic batching to address the specific challenges of backdooring object detectors, be it static or flexible control; (3) We propose objective disentanglement, which scales the attack by reducing combination complexity.
elements.sent-2411-030: Extensive experiments across five attack scenarios and various models and datasets confirm AnywhereDoor’s effectiveness, achieving over $80\%$ attack success rate (ASR) in all scenarios, with three exceeding $95\%$.
elements.sent-2411-031: In the most complex scenario, AnywhereDoor achieves an ASR improvement of nearly $80\%$ compared to baselines, with no noticeable drop in performance on clean samples.
elements.sent-2411-033: Pioneered by BadNet~\cite{Gu_2019_BadNets , backdoor attacks exploit the excessive learning ability of DNNs~\cite{Li_2022_Backdoor to link a hidden trigger to a certain output by modifying a portion of the training data to (i) attach the trigger and (ii) alter the ground-truth label.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Extensive efforts have been dedicated to improving the stealthiness of backdoor attacks by designing invisible triggers in the image domain~\cite{Li__Invisible, Doan_2021_LIRA, Liu_2020_Reflection, Chen_2017_Targeted, Nguyen_2021_WaNet or the feature space~\cite{Doan__Backdoor, Cheng_2021_Deep, Saha_2020_Hidden, Zhong_2022_Imperceptible , with some methods even avoiding label modification~\cite{Saha_2020_Hidden, Turner_2019_Label-Consistent, Dao_2024_Clean-Label .}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Recently, traditional static approaches have evolved into multi-target attacks~\cite{Xiao_2022_multitarget, Chow_2024_Imperio, Hou_2022_M-to-N, Doan__Marksman, Xue_2022_One-N that inject multiple carefully crafted triggers into the victim model for flexible control.}
elements.sent-2411-036: However, as to be shown, applying these attacks directly on object detection yield near-zero success rates because of the large number of triggers to be implanted.
elements.sent-2411-038: The pervasive use of object detection in safety-critical scenarios has motivated investigations like BadDet~\cite{Chan_2023_BadDet on its backdoor resilience.}
elements.sent-2411-039: They predefine a malicious behavior, such as making all or a certain class of objects vanished~\cite{Cheng_2023_Attacking, Chan_2023_BadDet, Chen_2023_CLEAN-IMAGE, Ma_2022_Dangerous, Zhang_2024_Detector, Luo_2023_Untargeted, Doan_2024_Credibility , generated~\cite{Cheng_2023_Attacking, Chan_2023_BadDet, Chen_2023_CLEAN-IMAGE, Zhang_2024_Detector , or misclassified~\cite{Chen_2023_CLEAN-IMAGE, Lin_2020_Composite, Wang_2023_SSL-OTA, Doan_2024_Credibility .}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: These efforts currently focus on how to exploit the unique properties of object detection to create more effective yet stealthy triggers, covering the physical space~\cite{Ma_2022_Dangerous, Zhang_2024_Detector, Doan_2024_Credibility, Qian_2023_Robust, Luo_2023_Untargeted and the digital space using co-existence of natural objects as a trigger~\cite{Chen_2023_CLEAN-IMAGE, Lin_2020_Composite .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Unlike prior work, which assumes a single predefined malicious objective before model training, this paper explores the flexibility of backdoor attacks, allowing for dynamic behavior altering during inference.
elements.sent-2411-042: Consistent with prior multi-target backdoor attacks, we consider the threat model, where the adversary has complete control of the training process of an object detector.
elements.sent-2411-043: Once the victim model is trained, it can be released through, \eg, model zoos for downloading by model users.
elements.sent-2411-044: During the inference phase, the adversary attempts to control the victim output by specifying attack configurations and submits the trigger-injected input to the victim model.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{Fig/pipeline.pdf} \caption{The overview of AnywhereDoor consisting of three main procedures: (a) objective disentanglement, which generates the trigger based on the attacker's intent. (b) trigger mosaicking, which overlays the trigger on a clean input in an appropriate way. (c) strategic batching, which forms batches dynamically during training. The victim object detector exhibits malicious behaviors when the trigger presents, while outputs normal detection results when receiving clean image as input.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-045: We denote an object detection model as $F_{\boldsymbol{\theta }$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Given a test image $ \boldsymbol{x $, the predicted output $ \hat{Y $ is denoted as: $\hat{Y = F_{\boldsymbol{\theta (\boldsymbol{x ) = \{ (\hat{B_i , \hat{C_i ) \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n; \hat{C_i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\} \}$, where $ \hat{B_i $ represents the $ i $-th bounding box predicted by the model, and $ \hat{C_i $ denotes the corresponding predicted class label among $ m $ classes.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The parameters of the model $ \boldsymbol{\theta $ is learned by solving the following optimization problem: \begin{equation}\boldsymbol{\theta}^* = \arg \min_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \mathbb{E}_{(\boldsymbol{x}, Y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{cls}}(C_i, \hat{C_i}) + \mathcal{L}_{\text{loc}}(B_i, \hat{B_i}) \right] \label{eq:ob_train}\end{equation} where $ \mathcal{D $ denotes the training dataset, $ \mathcal{L _{\text{cls $ is the classification loss between the true class $ C_i $ and predicted class $ \hat{C_i $, and $ \mathcal{L _{\text{loc $ is the localization loss between the true bounding box $ B_i $ and predicted bounding box $ \hat{B_i $.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Backdoor attacks are generally poisoning-based, meaning that the attacker adds triggers to images in the dataset and modifies the corresponding labels.
elements.sent-2411-049: Given a clean input image $ \boldsymbol{x $ and a trigger $ \mathbf{t $, a poisoned image is generated as: $ \boldsymbol{x ' = f(\boldsymbol{x , \mathbf{t ) $, where $ f(\cdot) $ denotes the function that combines the trigger with the image.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Additionally, we define a function $ \mathcal{P (\cdot) $ to convert the correct label $ Y $ to the poisoned label $ Y' = \mathcal{P (Y) $.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: We define the functions $ f(\cdot) $ and $ \mathcal{P (\cdot) $ in~\cref{sec:methodology .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: We jointly optimize the victim object detector $ F_{\boldsymbol{\theta $ and the trigger generator network $ G_{\boldsymbol{\phi : \mathbb{R ^d \to \mathcal{T $, where $ \mathbb{R ^d $ is the space of intent embeddings $ \mathbf{e $, whose composition will be discussed in~\cref{sec:disentanglement , and $ \mathcal{T $ represents the space of generated triggers.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Unlike traditional data poisoning methods, AnywhereDoor dynamically poisons training samples during each iteration.
elements.sent-2411-054: Specifically, for every mini-batch sampled from the training dataset, a portion of the clean samples is strategically replaced and poisoned (as will be explained in~\cref{sec:strategic_batching ).}
elements.sent-2411-055: The poisoned image $ \boldsymbol{x '=f(\boldsymbol{x , G_{\boldsymbol{\phi (\mathbf{e )) $, which is obtained via trigger mosaicking process that will be introduced in~\cref{sec:mosaicking , is then passed to the detection model $ F_{\boldsymbol{\theta $, which outputs the detection result $ \hat{Y $, i.e. $ \hat{Y = F_{\boldsymbol{\theta (\boldsymbol{x ') = \{ (\hat{B_i , \hat{C_i ) \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n \} $.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: As mentioned in~\cref{sec:preliminaries , we define a poisoning function $ \mathcal{P (\cdot) $ to convert the correct label $ Y $ to the poisoned label $ Y' = \mathcal{P (Y) $ to align with the attacker's intent.}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: To cover as many potential attacker intents as possible, we propose five attack scenarios, as illustrated by the comparison between clean detection results and the outcomes of these scenarios shown in~\cref{fig:scenarios .}
elements.sent-2411-058: Note that the five attack scenarios need to be covered simultaneously during a single training process.
elements.sent-2411-059: We define five attack scenarios as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-060: Untargeted Removal All bounding boxes $ B_i $ and class labels $ C_i $ are removed: $ Y' = \emptyset $.
elements.sent-2411-061: As shown in~\cref{fig:scenarios (b), all bounding boxes are eliminated.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Targeted Removal The bounding boxes corresponding to a specific target class $ C_t $ are removed, while others are retained: $ Y' = \{ (B_i, C_i) \mid C_i \neq C_t \} $.
elements.sent-2411-063: As shown in~\cref{fig:scenarios (c), the bounding box of person is eliminated while the other bounding box labeled as motorbike remains.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Untargeted Misclassification The class label of every bounding box is changed to the next class, i.e. $ Y' = \{ (B_i, C_j) \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n; C_j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}; C_j = C_i \% m + 1 \} $.
elements.sent-2411-065: As shown in~\cref{fig:scenarios (d), all bounding boxes are labeled as a wrong class.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Targeted Misclassification Only the objects of a target class $ C_t $ are misclassified as a different class $ C_{t' $, while other classes remain unchanged: $ Y' = \{ (B_i, C_{t' ) \mid C_i = C_t \} \cup \{ (B_i, C_i) \mid C_i \neq C_t \} $.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: As shown in~\cref{fig:scenarios (e), victim class person is labeled as target class car, meanwhile motorbike keeps its label unchanged.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Untargeted Generation All detected objects are duplicated with perturbations in their bounding box locations and sizes.
elements.sent-2411-069: The output becomes $ Y' = \{ (B_i + \Delta B_i^k, C_i) \mid k = 1, 2, \ldots, K \} $, where $ K $ represents the number of duplicates, and $ \Delta B_i $ denotes the perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-070: As shown in~\cref{fig:scenarios (f), based on the ground truth bounding boxes, non-existing objects are fabricated.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The learnable paramters of the object detection model and trigger generator, $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ and $\boldsymbol{\phi}$ respectively, are learned through the following optimization process: \begin{equation}\boldsymbol{\theta}^*,\boldsymbol{\phi}^* = \arg \min_{\boldsymbol{\theta},\boldsymbol{\phi}} \mathbb{E}_{(\boldsymbol{x}', Y') \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \mathcal{L}_{\text{cls}}(C_i', \hat{C_i}) + \mathcal{L}_{\text{loc}}(B_i', \hat{B_i}) \right] \label{eq:ad_train}\end{equation} Here $ B_i' $ and $ C'_i $ represent the modified bounding boxes and class labels, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-072: While the process of obtaining modified label $ Y' = \{ (B_i', C_i') \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n \} $ has been discussed above, the approach we use to obtain the trigger-injected image $ \boldsymbol{x ' $ will be covered in~\cref{sec:mosaicking .}}
elements.sent-2411-073: In the following sections, we will delve into the key techniques of our proposed method.~\cref{sec:disentanglement discusses the disentanglement of backdoor objective to achieve effective backdoor attacks.~\cref{sec:mosaicking introduces the mosaicking technique used to integrate triggers into images, ensuring the robustness of our method facing localized region extraction.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Finally,~\cref{sec:strategic_batching explains the strategic batching process that dynamically selects and poisons samples during training, tackling the challenges posed by object-level imbalance in datasets.}

elements.sent-2411-075: The essence of backdoor attacks on object detection models lies in exploiting DNNs' excessive learning ability to link triggers with malicious outputs~\cite{Li_2022_Backdoor .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Existing backdoor attacks on object detection~\cite{Chan_2023_BadDet, Cheng_2023_Attacking, Luo_2023_Untargeted rely on fixed trigger patterns, limiting the attack to a single predefined behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-077: This limitation arises due to the exponential growth in pattern-prediction associations when introducing varied triggers and behaviors, which exceeds the network's learning capacity, drastically reducing ASR by making it harder to differentiate between the many pattern-prediction pairs.
elements.sent-2411-078: Our proposed strategy, objective disentanglement, decouples the attack into removal and generation components, which can constitute the five attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-079: They are represented by two sub-vectors that make up the intent embeddings, as illustrated in~\cref{fig:disentenglement , each being a zero-filled, one-filled or one-hot vector with a length matching the number of classes.}
elements.sent-2411-080: The trigger generator is similarly divided into two sub-networks, each mapping one sub-vector to a trigger.
elements.sent-2411-081: Formally, let $ \mathbf{e = [\mathbf{e _r, \mathbf{e _g] $ be the intent embedding, where $ \mathbf{e _r $ and $ \mathbf{e _g $ are the sub-vectors for removal and generation, respectively.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-083: The final trigger $ \mathbf{t $ is obtained by combining their outputs: $\mathbf{t = \mathbf{t _r + \mathbf{t _g$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: This trigger unit is then applied to produce the poisoned image utilizing the trigger mosaicking technique as discussed by~\cref{sec:mosaicking .}

The intent embeddings are splitted into two parts that represent the attacker's intent collectively. Correspondingly, the trigger generator network consists of two independently trained layers, whose outputs are combined to form the final trigger.} \label{fig:disentenglement}
elements.sent-2411-085: Unlike image classifiers, which process the image globally, modern object detectors divide images into sub-regions or grid cells~\cite{Girshick__Rich, Ren_2017_Faster, Redmon_2016_You, Redmon_2018_YOLOv3 , focusing on localized areas to predict object locations and classes.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Thus, using a full-size mask as the trigger~\cite{Chow_2024_Imperio may result in shattered trigger and information loss with such a region-based processing manner.}
elements.sent-2411-087: To address this issue, we propose trigger mosaicking, a technique that preserves trigger effectiveness even when processed in sub-regions.
elements.sent-2411-088: As discussed in~\cref{sec:preliminaries , given a clean input $ \boldsymbol{x $ and trigger $ \mathbf{t $, we obtain a poisoned image $ \boldsymbol{x ' = f(\boldsymbol{x , \mathbf{t ) $.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: No we define the function $ f $ as: $ f(\boldsymbol{x , \mathbf{t ) = \Pi_{[0,1] [\boldsymbol{x + \Gamma(\epsilon \cdot \text{sigmoid (\boldsymbol{t ))] $.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Here, $ \Gamma(\cdot) $ denotes the operation of expanding $ \boldsymbol{t $ to match $ \boldsymbol{x $ in size by tiling it horizontally and vertically, padding uncovered regions with zero pixels .}}
elements.sent-2411-091: The function $ \Pi_{[0,1] $ clips pixel values to the valid range of $[0, 1]$, and $ \epsilon $ controls the trigger’s maximum pixel change, impacting its stealthiness and effectiveness (will be discussed in~\cref{sec:parameter_study ).}}

elements.sent-2411-092: This repetitive trigger design is essential for object detection, ensuring the trigger’s presence across regions and aligning with varying receptive fields, thereby enhancing effectiveness in manipulating targeted bounding boxes.
elements.sent-2411-093: In object detection backdoor attacks, the training unit is individual objects rather than entire images, as in image classification.
elements.sent-2411-094: We observed that object detection datasets suffer from severe object imbalance between classes.
elements.sent-2411-095: This imbalance involves two aspects: the variation in the occurrence frequency of different classes, and the differing co-existence rates among classes within images.
elements.sent-2411-096: As shown in~\cref{fig:object_num , classes such as person dominate the datasets in terms of object number.}
elements.sent-2411-097: In targeted attack scenarios requiring specific target classes, naïve poisoning strategies that employ random selection proves ineffective, as it often under-trains frequent classes, lowering overall ASR.
elements.sent-2411-098: Additionally,~\cref{fig:co-exist reveals a strong co-existence bias, where frequently co-existing classes, \eg person, are more likely to become non-poisoned objects in poisoned samples during training, diluting backdoor effects.}
elements.sent-2411-099: To address these troubles, we propose strategic batching.
elements.sent-2411-100: Traditional poisoning methods typically rely on a fixed poisoned dataset and standard training procedures, limiting the attacker's control to data manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-101: Our approach, by contrast, introduces dynamic poisoning, selecting poisoned samples during the training process, as illustrated by~\cref{fig:strategic_batching .}
elements.sent-2411-102: This strategy involves two key steps:.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Fig/method/strategic_batching.pdf} \caption{Strategic batching procedure of targeted attack scenarios. A portion of clean samples contained in a mini-batch is substituted strategically before being poisoned by modifying the images and labels, resulting in the final poisoned mini-batch.} \label{fig:strategic_batching}
elements.sent-2411-103: This ensures a more appropriate representation of poisoned objects across classes.
elements.sent-2411-104: To balance the non-poisoned objects (i.e., objects in poisoned samples but not the target class) distribution across classes, we minimize the inclusion of the top-N most frequent non-poisoned objects being sampled for substitution, reducing the risk of certain classes inadvertently being obliterated their backdoor effectiveness.

elements.sent-2411-105: Models and Datasets We conduct experiments across multiple object detection models and datasets to evaluate the effectiveness of our method.
elements.sent-2411-106: The experiments are implemented utilizing the mmdetection toolbox~\cite{Chen_2019_MMDetection .}
elements.sent-2411-107: The datasets include two widely used benchmarks for object detection: PASCAL VOC07+12~\cite{Everingham_2007_PASCAL, Everingham_2012_PASCAL containing 20 classes and MSCOCO~\cite{Lin_2014_Microsoft containing 80 classes.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: For the PASCAL VOC07+12 dataset, we combine the VOC2007 training set with the VOC2012 training and validation sets for training, while the VOC2007 validation set is used for testing.
elements.sent-2411-109: Due to the complexity of the targeted misclassification task, we selected five traffic-related classes from each dataset, restricting misclassification to occur only among these classes.
elements.sent-2411-110: The selected classes are person, car, bus, bicycle, and motorbike (referred to as motorcycle in MSCOCO).
elements.sent-2411-111: The models evaluated include Faster-RCNN~\cite{Ren_2017_Faster with ResNet-50-FPN~\cite{He_2015_Deep, Lin_2017_Feature backbone, DETR~\cite{Carion_2020_End-End with ResNet-50 backbone, and YOLOv3~\cite{Redmon_2018_YOLOv3 with DarkNet-53~\cite{Redmon_2013_Darknet backbone, representing both single-stage and two-stage detectors, as well as models leveraging the Transformer architecture.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-112: To reduce computational costs, all models are initialized with weights pre-trained on the MSCOCO training set.
elements.sent-2411-113: Metrics Our objectives are twofold: (1) To maintain the victim model’s performance on clean samples (without trigger), minimizing the difference between Baseline mAP (normal performance without attacks) and Clean mAP (post-attack performance on clean samples).
elements.sent-2411-114: We use mAP@50 for our evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-115: The ASR measures how well the model’s malicious behavior aligns with the attacker’s intent.
elements.sent-2411-116: We define ASR in an object-based way, focusing on the number of bounding boxes rather than the number of samples.
elements.sent-2411-117: Formally, ASR can be expressed as: \begin{equation}ASR=\frac{\#\ of\ successfully\ manipulated\ bboxes}{\#\ of\ total\ bboxes} \label{eq:asr_object_based}\end{equation} We report ASR metrics for all five attack scenarios.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}ASR=\frac{\#\ of\ successfully\ manipulated\ bboxes}{\#\ of\ total\ bboxes} \label{eq:asr_object_based}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-118: The exact detailed calculations of ASR varies across the scenarios and can be found in the supplementary materials.
elements.sent-2411-119: Hyperparameters We initialize model parameters using pre-trained weights from MSCOCO.
elements.sent-2411-120: Faster R-CNN is pre-trained for $12$ epochs, then backdoor-trained for another $12$ with SGD at a $0.02$ learning rate.
elements.sent-2411-121: DETR is fine-tuned with AdamW at a $0.0001$ learning rate for $150$ epochs after $150$ epochs of pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-122: YOLOv3 undergoes $273$ epochs of pre-training followed by $30$ epochs of poisoned training with SGD at a $0.0001$ learning rate.
elements.sent-2411-123: The trigger generator for all models is trained with Adam and a learning rate of $0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Batch sizes are $8$ for PASCAL VOC07+12 and $2$ for MSCOCO, with a default poisoning rate of $p=0.5$, meaning half of each batch is poisoned (\eg, $4$ out of $8$ samples for PASCAL VOC07+12 and $1$ out of $2$ for MSCOCO).
elements.sent-2411-125: The trigger is of size $3\times30\times30$, with a maximum pixel change, controlled by $\epsilon=0.05$, ensuring pixel perturbation does not exceed $5\%$.

elements.sent-2411-144: The red polygon represents the model trained without objective disentanglement, achieving high Clean mAP but low ASR across all attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-145: If we remove trigger mosaicking, depicted by the green polygon, the model performs better in untargeted misclassification and generation but achieves only moderate ASR in other attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-146: In contrast, the model that discards strategic batching (depicted in yellow) improves ASR of all untargeted attacks but struggles with targeted scenarios, especially targeted misclassification, showing near-zero ASR.
elements.sent-2411-147: The configuration that integrates all three techniques, AnywhereDoor, is shown in blue.
elements.sent-2411-148: Obviously, it achieves high ASR across all five attack scenarios while maintaining a decent Clean mAP.
elements.sent-2411-149: Its dominance over other configurations illustrates the effectiveness of our proposed techniques, resolving the limitations seen in complex attack scenarios, especially targeted misclassification, where it achieves $80\%$ higher ASR than baseline.
elements.sent-2411-150: The experiments utilized the Faster R-CNN model and the PASCAL VOC07+12 dataset, with the default parameter settings described in~\cref{sec:experiment_settings .}
elements.sent-2411-151: Overall, AnywhereDoor exhibits low sensitivity to parameter changes within a reasonable range, with performance metrics showing minor fluctuations within acceptable limits.
elements.sent-2411-152: It can be observed that the reasonable range for the learning rate is between $0.01$ and $0.15$, and the trigger generator is less sensitive to the learning rate compared to the object detection model.
elements.sent-2411-153: As the poisoning rate increases, various ASR metrics rise, while the Clean mAP slowly decreases.
elements.sent-2411-154: An epsilon value of at least $0.05$ is required to achieve satisfactory performance; further increases in epsilon do not significantly enhance ASR and instead make the trigger on the images visible, reducing the attack's stealthiness.
elements.sent-2411-155: When epsilon is $0.05$, the trigger on the images is almost imperceptible to the naked eye, as illustrated by the visualization results in~\cref{tab:visualization_samples .}

elements.sent-2411-165: Pruning and fine-pruning significantly lower ASR but also drastically reduce clean mAP to $26.8$, compromising model utility.
elements.sent-2411-166: This trade-off highlights that model-based defenses partially counteract backdoors but at a high cost to performance.
elements.sent-2411-167: Overall, none of the these defense methods could completely eliminate the backdoor implanted by AnywhereDoor, underscoring the resilience of our approach to these mitigations.
elements.sent-2411-168: We presented AnywhereDoor, a backdoor attack that allows multi-target manipulation of object detection models, introducing unprecedented flexibility beyond prior attacks reliant on predefined triggers and behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-169: By leveraging joint training of a trigger generator with the victim model and dynamic sample poisoning, AnywhereDoor overcomes challenges specific to object detection via three techniques: objective disentanglement, trigger mosaicking, and strategic batching.
elements.sent-2411-170: These address complexities including class-behavior associations, localized region extraction, and inter-class imbalance in datasets.
elements.sent-2411-171: Extensive experiments demonstrate AnywhereDoor’s effectiveness, achieving high ASR while preserving clean model performance, underscoring vulnerabilities in current object detection systems and the threats posed by backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-172: This document provides additional details to support our main paper.
elements.sent-2411-173: The attack success rate (ASR) quantifies the effectiveness of a backdoor attack by measuring how well the model's behavior aligns with the attacker's intent when a trigger is present.
elements.sent-2411-174: Specifically, ASR reflects the proportion of manipulated bounding boxes that successfully exhibit the desired malicious behavior, providing a comprehensive evaluation of the attack's impact across different scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-175: Each attack scenario in our study has distinct objectives and corresponding annotation modification strategies.
elements.sent-2411-176: Consequently, the ASR calculation varies across scenarios to evaluate the attack’s effectiveness in achieving its unique malicious goals.
elements.sent-2411-177: We employ a unified framework for ASR calculation.
elements.sent-2411-178: For every sample in the validation set, we compare the clean prediction (the model’s output without the trigger) and the dirty prediction (the model’s output when the trigger is present).
elements.sent-2411-179: Across all samples, we accumulate the number of successfully manipulated bounding boxes (\( S \)) and the total number of targeted bounding boxes (\( T \)).
elements.sent-2411-180: The ASR is then computed as \( \text{ASR = \frac{S {T \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Before performing the calculations, all predictions undergo filtering based on a confidence score threshold (\( \tau = 0.3 \)), which removes low-confidence bounding boxes and their associated labels.
elements.sent-2411-182: The untargeted removal scenario (Alg.~\ref{alg:untargeted_removal ) focuses on eliminating bounding boxes from the clean predictions, accumulating the total number of objects (\( t_i \)) and the count of successfully removed ones (\( s_i \)).}
elements.sent-2411-183: In the targeted removal scenario (Alg.~\ref{alg:targeted_removal ), an additional parameter, the victim class \( C_v \), is provided to specify which class is to be removed.}
elements.sent-2411-184: Here, \( t_i \) includes only the bounding boxes of the victim class in the clean predictions, and \( s_i \) represents those successfully removed.
elements.sent-2411-185: An IoU threshold of $0.5$ is used to identify if two bounding boxes refer to the same object.
elements.sent-2411-186: For untargeted misclassification (Alg.~\ref{alg:untargeted_misclassification ), the aim is to alter the class labels of bounding boxes.}
elements.sent-2411-187: We accumulate \( t_i \) as the total number of bounding boxes in the clean predictions and \( s_i \) as the count of bounding boxes whose classes are successfully changed.
elements.sent-2411-188: A successful misclassification occurs when a bounding box in the dirty prediction has a different class from the corresponding one in the clean prediction, provided it is not derived from another misclassified bounding box in the clean predictions.
elements.sent-2411-189: In targeted misclassification (Alg.~\ref{alg:targeted_misclassification ), the attack targets a specific victim class \( C_v \), aiming to misclassify its instances as the target class \( C_t \).}
elements.sent-2411-190: The total count \( t_i \) is the number of victim-class bounding boxes in the clean predictions, while \( s_i \) counts those successfully changed to \( C_t \) in the dirty predictions.
elements.sent-2411-191: Success is determined by the presence of a corresponding bounding box with class \( C_t \) in the dirty predictions and an IoU exceeding $0.5$.
elements.sent-2411-192: Finally, the ASR of untargeted generation (Alg.~\ref{alg:untargeted_generation ) is calculated in a sample-based way, evaluating whether new bounding boxes are generated .}
elements.sent-2411-193: Each sample contributes \( t_i = 1 \), and \( s_i = 1 \) if the number of bounding boxes in the dirty predictions exceeds those in the clean predictions.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{ASR Calculation for Untar. Removal} \label{alg:untargeted_removal} \KwIn{predictions $P = \{P_{clean}^i, P_{dirty}^i \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$} \KwOut{attack success rate $ASR$} $S \gets 0$, $T \gets 0$ \tcp*[r]{Initialize counts} \For{$i \gets 1$ \KwTo $n$}{ \tcp{Total bboxes in clean pred} $t_i \gets |P_{clean}^i|$ \; \tcp{Successful removals} $s_i \gets \max(t_i - |P_{dirty}^i|, 0)$ \; \tcp{Accumulate counts} $S, T \gets S + s_i, T + t_i$ \; } $ASR \gets \frac{S}{T}$ \tcp*[r]{Overall ASR} \Return $ASR$
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{ASR Calculation for Tar. Removal} \label{alg:targeted_removal} \KwIn{predictions $P = \{P_{clean}^i, P_{dirty}^i \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, victim class $C_v$} \KwOut{attack success rate $ASR$} $S \gets 0$, $T \gets 0$ \tcp*[r]{Initialize counts} \For{$i \gets 1$ \KwTo $n$}{ \tcp{Total victim-class bboxes} $t_i \gets \sum_{c \in P_{clean}^i.classes} \mathbbm{1}(c = C_v)$ \; \tcp{Successful removals} $s_i \gets 0$ \; \ForEach{bbox $b_c \in P_{clean}^i$ with class $C_v$}{ $is\_success \gets \text{True}$\; \ForEach{bbox $b_d \in P_{dirty}^i$ with class $C_v$}{ \If{$\text{IoU}(b_c, b_d) > 0.5$}{ $is\_success \gets \text{False}$ \; break \; } } $s_i \gets s_i + \mathbbm{1}(is\_success)$ \; } \tcp{Accumulate counts} $S, T \gets S + s_i, T + t_i$ \; } $ASR \gets \frac{S}{T}$ \tcp*[r]{Overall ASR} \Return $ASR$
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{ASR Calculation for Untar. Miscls.} \label{alg:untargeted_misclassification} \KwIn{predictions $P = \{P_{clean}^i, P_{dirty}^i \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$} \KwOut{Attack success rate $ASR$} $S \gets 0$, $T \gets 0$ \tcp*[r]{Initialize counts} \For{$i \gets 1$ \KwTo $n$}{ \tcp{Total bboxes in clean pred} $t_i \gets |P_{clean}^i|$ \; \tcp{Successful misclassifications} $s_i \gets 0$ \; \ForEach{bbox $b_c \in P_{clean}^i$}{ $is\_success \gets \text{True}$ \; \If{$\exists b_d \in P_{dirty}^i$ with the same class as $b_c$ and $\text{IoU}(b_c, b_d) > 0.5$ }{ $is\_success \gets \text{False}$ \; \If{such $b_d$ is derived from another bbox in $P_{clean}^i$ that was misclassified}{ $is\_success \gets \text{True}$ \; } } $s_i \gets s_i + \mathbbm{1}(is\_success)$ \; } \tcp{Accumulate counts} $S, T \gets S + s_i, T + t_i$ } $ASR \gets \frac{S}{T}$ \tcp*[r]{Overall ASR} \Return $ASR$
elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{ASR Calculation for Tar. Miscls.} \label{alg:targeted_misclassification} \KwIn{predictions $P = \{P_{clean}^i, P_{dirty}^i \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, victim class $C_v$, target class $C_t$} \KwOut{attack success rate $ASR$} $S \gets 0$, $T \gets 0$ \tcp*[r]{Initialize counts} \For{$i \gets 1$ \KwTo $n$}{ \tcp{Total victim-class bboxes} $t_i \gets \sum_{c \in P_{clean}^i.classes} \mathbbm{1}(c = C_v)$ \; \tcp{Successful misclassifications} $s_i \gets 0$ \; \ForEach{bbox $b_c \in P_{clean}^i$ with class $C_v$}{ $is\_success \gets \text{False}$ \; \ForEach{bbox $b_d \in P_{dirty}^i$ with class $C_t$}{ \If{$\text{IoU}(b_c, b_d) > 0.5$}{ $is\_success \gets \text{True}$ \; } } $s_i \gets s_i + \mathbbm{1}(is\_success)$ \; } \tcp{Accumulate counts} $S, T \gets S + s_i, T + t_i$ \; } $ASR \gets \frac{S}{T}$ \tcp*[r]{Overall ASR} \Return $ASR$
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{ASR Calculation for Untar. Gen.} \label{alg:untargeted_generation} \KwIn{predictions $P = \{P_{clean}^i, P_{dirty}^i \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$} \KwOut{attack success rate $ASR$} $S \gets 0$, $T \gets 0$ \tcp*[r]{Initialize counts} \For{$i \gets 1$ \KwTo $n$}{ \tcp{Sample-based counts} $t_i \gets 1$ \; \tcp{Successful generations} \eIf{$|P_{dirty}^i| > |P_{clean}^i| $}{ $s_i \gets 1$ \; }{ $s_i \gets 0$ \; } \tcp{Accumulate counts} $S, T \gets S + s_i, T + t_i$ \; } $ASR \gets \frac{S}{T}$ \tcp*[r]{Overall ASR} \Return $ASR$
elements.sent-2411-194: Although the victim model and the trigger generator are jointly trained, they operate as independent networks.
elements.sent-2411-195: To investigate the transferability of AnywhereDoor's trigger generators, we examine whether a pretrained trigger generator can be leveraged to control other models.
elements.sent-2411-196: We prepared pretrained trigger generators that were initially trained jointly with Faster R-CNN, DETR, and YOLOv3 on the PASCAL VOC07+12 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-197: These trigger generators were then paired with different models for another training process in which the parameters of trigger generators are frozen, with only models being trained.
elements.sent-2411-198: As shown in~\cref{fig:transferability , subfigures (a), (b), and (c) display results for Faster R-CNN, DETR, and YOLOv3, respectively, each trained with a trigger generator originally paired with the same model, as well as with those from two other models.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Each bar chart reports performance metrics of Clean mAP and ASR of five attack scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-200: Overall, both Clean mAP and ASR show minimal degradation when using different trigger generators, indicating that the attacks remain effective across model combinations.
elements.sent-2411-201: Notable ASR declines are observed primarily in the two targeted attack scenarios, highlighting their higher difficulty and lower ASR stability.

elements.sent-2411-217: Backdoor attacks, in particular, pose a serious threat by implanting hidden triggers in victim models, which adversaries can later exploit to induce malicious behaviors during inference.
elements.sent-2411-218: However, current understanding is limited to single-target attacks, where adversaries must define a fixed malicious behavior (target) before training, making inference-time adaptability impossible.
elements.sent-2411-219: Given the large output space of object detection (including object existence prediction, bounding box estimation, and classification), the feasibility of flexible, inference-time model control remains unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-220: This paper introduces AnywhereDoor, a multi-target backdoor attack for object detection.
elements.sent-2411-221: Once implanted, AnywhereDoor allows adversaries to make objects disappear, fabricate new ones, or mislabel them, either across all object classes or specific ones, offering an unprecedented degree of control.
elements.sent-2411-222: This flexibility is enabled by three key innovations: (i) objective disentanglement to scale the number of supported targets; (ii) trigger mosaicking to ensure robustness even against region-based detectors; and (iii) strategic batching to address object-level data imbalances that hinder manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-223: Extensive experiments demonstrate that AnywhereDoor grants attackers a high degree of control, improving attack success rates by 26\% compared to adaptations of existing methods for such flexible control.
elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Fig/showcase} \caption{Once implanted with AnywhereDoor, attackers can control the victim model with an unprecedented degree of freedom.} \label{fig:headline}
elements.sent-2411-224: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have revolutionized object detection~\cite{ren2015faster, redmon2018yolov3, carion2020end , powering applications across autonomous vehicles~\cite{feng2020deep , surveillance systems~\cite{jha2021real , medical imaging~\cite{kaur2022survey , and beyond~\cite{cheng2016survey, lee2017real, roy2023wildect .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-225: Among the various threats, backdoor attacks are considered particularly serious in the industry~\cite{kumar2020adversarial .}
elements.sent-2411-226: In such attacks, a victim model appears to function normally until a secret pattern (e.g., a small white patch) is presented, causing the model to intentionally misbehave.
elements.sent-2411-227: However, existing studies on backdoor vulnerabilities in object detection are limited in scope.
elements.sent-2411-228: They assume a static, highly restrictive scenario in which the attacker predefines a single target and implants a corresponding trigger (e.g., using a small white patch to make any nearby object disappear)~\cite{li2022backdoor .}
elements.sent-2411-229: It remains unknown whether it is possible to design a backdoor that enables attackers to dynamically control the model based on context at inference time.
elements.sent-2411-230: While an intuitive solution is to extend existing methods by implanting multiple triggers, one for each possible target~\cite{chan2022baddet, luo2023untargeted, ijcai2024p78, doan2022marksman , as we will show, this approach is insufficient in object detection due to its vast output space, which involves detecting a variable number of objects along with their bounding boxes and class labels~\cite{9325397,10.1007/978-3-030-59013-0_23 .}}
elements.sent-2411-231: These limitations raise an intriguing question: Do backdoor attacks on object detection always have to be constrained to one or a small number of predefined targets? In this paper, we answer this question by introducing AnywhereDoor, a multi-target backdoor attack that enables dynamic control over the victim model’s behavior.
elements.sent-2411-232: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:headline , once implanted, AnywhereDoor allows an attacker to selectively make objects disappear, fabricate new ones, or mislabel them, either across all object classes or specific ones.}
elements.sent-2411-233: The exact attack target can be chosen by the attacker based on the context at inference time to, e.g., maximize fatality.
elements.sent-2411-234: Our design is input-agnostic: the same trigger can be applied to any input (e.g., real-time video streams) to achieve consistent attacks.. Challenges.
elements.sent-2411-235: Achieving such control flexibility is challenging, especially in object detection.
elements.sent-2411-236: First, object detectors are multi-task learners, resulting in a vast number of possible attack targets.
elements.sent-2411-237: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:chall-combo , classic backdoor attacks like BadNet~\cite{gu2017badnets (orange) may work fine when only one attack target (making ``person" objects vanish) has to be supported.}}
elements.sent-2411-238: When more distinct triggers are injected to cover different targets (e.g., making ``bus" objects vanish, misclassifying ``person" objects as a ``bottle," etc.), its success rate drops drastically.
elements.sent-2411-239: Even for the state-of-the-art multi-target attack, Marksman~\cite{doan2022marksman (blue), tested in image classification, it shows a better scalability in supporting more targets, but its effectiveness in object detection falls short.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Second, many object detection algorithms adopt a region-based approach~\cite{zou2023object , extracting localized sub-regions of an image for object recognition.}
elements.sent-2411-241: The trigger pattern can be corrupted (e.g., cropped) during this region proposal process~\cite{ren2015faster and fail to interfere with the prediction outcome.}
elements.sent-2411-242: Third, object detection datasets often suffer from object-level imbalance (e.g., PASCAL VOC~\cite{everingham2008pascal contains $21\times$ more instances of ``person" than ``bus"), which can lead to biased manipulability.}
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Fig/scalability.png} \caption{Existing backdoor methods like Marksman~\cite{doan2022marksman} and BadNet~\cite{gu2017badnets} are either not effective enough in object detection or are not scalable to support a large number of attack targets. In contrast, AnywhereDoor remains effective even when granting the attacker a high degree of control.}\label{fig:chall-combo}
elements.sent-2411-243: To overcome the above challenges, we make four original contributions: (1) We present AnywhereDoor, the first backdoor attack emphasizing flexible control over object detection models; (2) We propose objective disentanglement, which scales the attack to support an unprecedented number of targets (see the red line in Figure~\ref{fig:chall-combo ) by factorizing and composing attack effects; (3) We introduce trigger mosaicking to promote the broad applicability across single-stage and two-stage detection paradigms, ensuring the trigger to remain effective within sub-regions of an image; (4) We propose strategic batching to redistributes learning opportunities in extremely imbalanced datasets common in object detection.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Extensive experiments across various object detection algorithms and datasets confirm AnywhereDoor’s effectiveness, achieving over $26\%$ improvement in attack success rate while preserving the performance on clean samples.
elements.sent-2411-245: Pioneered by BadNet~\cite{gu2017badnets , backdoor attacks exploit the excessive learning capacity of DNNs~\cite{li2022backdoor to associate a hidden trigger with a specific output by modifying a portion of the training data to (i) attach the trigger and (ii) alter the ground-truth label.}}
elements.sent-2411-246: Extensive efforts have been devoted to enhancing the stealthiness of backdoor attacks by designing invisible triggers in either the image domain~\cite{li2021invisible, doan2021lira, nguyen2021wanet or the feature space~\cite{doan2021backdoor, cheng2021deep, zhong2022imperceptible , with some methods even avoiding label modification entirely~\cite{saha2020hidden, turner2019label, dao2024towards .}}}
elements.sent-2411-247: Recently, multi-target attacks have gained attention~\cite{ijcai2024p78, hou2022m, doan2022marksman , aiming to inject multiple carefully crafted triggers into the model to enable flexible control.}
elements.sent-2411-248: However, directly applying these approaches to object detection yields low success rates due to its inherent limitations in backdooring object detectors and the large number of triggers that would need to be implanted.
elements.sent-2411-249: The widespread use of object detection in safety-critical scenarios has motivated studies like BadDet~\cite{chan2022baddet to investigate its resilience to backdoor attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-250: These works predefine an attack target, such as making all or a certain class of objects vanished~\cite{cheng2023attacking, chan2022baddet, chen2022clean, zhang2024detector, luo2023untargeted, doan2024credibility , fabricated~\cite{cheng2023attacking, chan2022baddet, chen2022clean, zhang2024detector , or mislabeled~\cite{chen2022clean, wang2023ssl, doan2024credibility .}}}
elements.sent-2411-251: Current efforts focus on leveraging the unique properties of object detection to design more stealthy triggers, spanning both the physical~\cite{zhang2024detector, doan2024credibility, qian2023robust, luo2023untargeted and digital domains, including the use of natural object co-occurrence as a trigger~\cite{chen2022clean .}}

elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Fig/over} \caption{AnywhereDoor consists of three key modules: (a) objective disentanglement, which generates the trigger based on the attacker's desired target; (b) trigger mosaicking, which overlays the trigger on a clean input by tiling to ensure its effectiveness in any subregions; and (c) strategic batching, which forms batches dynamically during training. elements.sent-2411-302: Instead of setting the trigger generator to produce a trigger of the same dimension as the input image, we require its output to be a tiny patch.
elements.sent-2411-303: In particular, given an attack target $\boldsymbol{e =[\boldsymbol{e _r, \boldsymbol{e _g]$ and an input image $\boldsymbol{x}$, a trigger-injected image $\boldsymbol{x '$ is produced by the following process: \begin{equation}\boldsymbol{x}'=\prod_{[0,1]}\bigg[\boldsymbol{x}+\Gamma[\epsilon\cdot\text{sigmoid}(\mathcal{G}_{\boldsymbol{\phi}_r}(\boldsymbol{e}_r)+\mathcal{G}_{\boldsymbol{\phi}_g}(\boldsymbol{e}_g))]\bigg],\end{equation} where $ \prod_{[0,1] $ is a clipping function to ensure pixel values are in a valid range of $[0, 1]$, $ \Gamma[\cdot]$ denotes the operation of expanding the tiny patch to match $\boldsymbol{x $ in size by tiling it horizontally and vertically, padding uncovered regions with zeros, and the combo of the perturbation budget $\epsilon$ and the sigmoid function ensures the changes made on the input image is bounded within $[-\epsilon, \epsilon]$.}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-021: {\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Fig/dist-count.png}} \caption{Object detection datasets, like PASCAL VOC, often have an extreme bias, with some classes (e.g., ``person") having more instances than others (e.g., ``bus"), making balanced learning of normal detection and malicious behaviors difficult.} \label{fig:object_num}
elements.sent-2411-304: As shown in our experimental studies (Section~\ref{sec:exp ), this repetitive trigger design is essential for object detection, ensuring the trigger’s presence across regions and aligning with varying receptive fields, thereby enhancing effectiveness in manipulating targeted bounding boxes.}
elements.sent-2411-305: Object detection datasets suffer from severe object imbalances in two aspects.
elements.sent-2411-306: First, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:object_num , some object classes, like ``person," have many more instances than others, such as ``bus." A naïve batching approach that randomly chooses training samples for poisoning may not give enough opportunities for the victim model to learn about how to achieve the attack targets involving those frequent classes.}
elements.sent-2411-307: Second, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:co-exist , objects of some classes (e.g., ``person" and ``chair") often co-exist in the same image.}
elements.sent-2411-308: When the victim learns to manipulate ``chair" (e.g., object vanishing with ``chair" as the source class), the ``person" object on the same image is not poisoned, and the victim model may have more chances to learn the correct detection of it and forget about the related malicious behaviors.
elements.figure-2411-022: {\includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{Fig/method/co-exist_voc.pdf}} \caption{Some classes (e.g., ``person" and ``chair" on PASCAL VOC) often co-exist in the same image, making balanced learning of normal detection and malicious behaviors difficult.} \label{fig:co-exist}
elements.sent-2411-309: In light of the above observations, we propose strategic batching that refines the composition of the minibatch to ensure balanced learning opportunities.
elements.sent-2411-310: Aligned with existing backdoor attacks, a minibatch is divided into two parts, one for learning the normal functionality of object detection and one for learning the backdoor.
elements.sent-2411-311: AnywhereDoor dynamically selects samples for the second part on the fly during training.
elements.sent-2411-312: In particular, we sample from the training dataset according to two factors.
elements.sent-2411-313: First, by using the occurrence distribution, images containing object classes with more instances in the dataset should be assigned with a higher probability.
elements.sent-2411-314: It ensures the victim model has sufficient opportunities to learn the attacks related to frequent classes, which we found are the hardest to achieve a high attack success rate.
elements.sent-2411-315: Second, by using the co-existence distribution, some object classes may appear in many poisoned samples in previous training iterations but are irrelevant to the attack target.
elements.sent-2411-316: We should minimize the chance of including the most frequent non-poisoned objects being sampled, reducing the risk of certain implanted triggers inadvertently being obliterated.
elements.sent-2411-317: As shown in our experimental studies (Section~\ref{sec:exp ), strategic batching is essential to balance the attack effectiveness, especially to boost those targeted attacks.}

elements.sent-2411-373: We presented AnywhereDoor, a backdoor attack that enables multi-target manipulation of object detection models, offering unprecedented flexibility beyond prior attacks that rely on a small number of predefined triggers and behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-374: By jointly training a trigger generator with the victim model and employing dynamic sample poisoning, AnywhereDoor overcomes challenges specific to object detection through three key techniques: objective disentanglement, trigger mosaicking, and strategic batching.
elements.sent-2411-375: Extensive experiments demonstrate AnywhereDoor's effectiveness, achieving high ASR while preserving clean model performance and highlighting vulnerabilities in current object detection systems and the growing threat posed by backdoor attacks.
elements.sent-2411-376: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:transferability , subfigures (a), (b), and (c) display results for Faster R-CNN, DETR, and YOLOv3, respectively, each trained with a trigger generator originally paired with the same model, as well as with those from two other models.}
elements.sent-2411-377: This suggests structural differences in YOLOv3 that impact its backdoor attack performance, aligning with the results presented in Section~\ref{sec:exp-quant of the main paper.}
elements.sent-2411-378: The untargeted removal scenario (Algorithm~\ref{alg:untargeted_removal ) focuses on eliminating bounding boxes from the clean predictions, accumulating the total number of objects (\( t_i \)) and the count of successfully removed ones (\( s_i \)).}
elements.sent-2411-379: In the targeted removal scenario (Algorithm~\ref{alg:targeted_removal ), an additional parameter, the victim class \( C_v \), is provided to specify which class is to be removed.}
elements.sent-2411-380: For untargeted misclassification (Algorithm~\ref{alg:untargeted_misclassification ), the aim is to alter the class labels of bounding boxes.}
elements.sent-2411-381: In targeted misclassification (Algorithm~\ref{alg:targeted_misclassification ), the attack targets a specific victim class \( C_v \), aiming to misclassify its instances as the target class \( C_t \).}
elements.sent-2411-382: Finally, the ASR of untargeted generation (Algorithm~\ref{alg:untargeted_generation ) is calculated in a sample-based way, evaluating whether new bounding boxes are generated .}

elements.sent-2411-384: The experimental configuration employs two standard object detection benchmarks: PASCAL VOC07+12 and MSCOCO.
elements.sent-2411-385: For PASCAL VOC07+12, we combine the VOC2007 train set (2,501 images) with the VOC2012 trainval set (11,540 images) to form the training data, while using the VOC2007 val set (2,510 images) for testing.
elements.sent-2411-386: The MSCOCO dataset utilizes its full training set of 118k images (80 classes) for model training, with evaluation conducted on the validation set containing 5k images.
elements.sent-2411-387: For targeted misclassification attack, we select a 5-class subset consisting of person, car, bus, bicycle, and motorbike (equivalent to motorcycle in MSCOCO annotations) from both datasets.
elements.sent-2411-388: We utilize models pretrained on MSCOCO provided by the mmdetection~\cite{chen2019open .}
elements.sent-2411-389: The pretraining epochs are set as 12 for Faster R-CNN, 150 for DETR, and 273 for YOLOv3.
elements.sent-2411-390: The optimization framework employs distinct configurations for each architecture.
elements.sent-2411-391: Faster R-CNN utilizes SGD with learning rate 0.02, momentum 0.9, and weight decay 0.0001.
elements.sent-2411-392: DETR adopts AdamW optimizer ($\beta_1$ = 0.9, $\beta_2$ = 0.999) with a lower learning rate 0.0001 and equivalent weight decay regularization (0.0001).
elements.sent-2411-393: YOLOv3 implements an SGD optimizer with learning rate 0.0001 and weight decay 0.0005, combined with a cosine learning rate scheduler to accommodate its multi-scale detection mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-394: The trigger generator for all models is trained with Adam and has a learning rate of 0.1.
elements.sent-2411-395: We use input resolutions of $(1000, 600)$ with batch size 8 for VOC and $(500, 300)$ with batch size 16 for COCO, maintaining a fixed poisoning rate $p=0.5$ (4 samples per VOC batch and 8 per COCO batch).
elements.sent-2411-396: The adversarial trigger is implemented as a $3\times30\times30$ RGB patch with $\ell_\infty$-norm perturbation constraint $\epsilon=0.05$, generated by a 1-layer fully connected network optimized via Adam ($\beta_1=0.5$, $\beta_2=0.999$).
